text: AI just hit the fastforward button this month. Bite Dance just dropped Cance 2.0 and it's generating cinematic 1080p videos so realistic and polished that Hollywood is reportedly panicking after seeing the results. Open AAI just launched Codeex Spark for instant coding. Google dropped Gemini 3 Deep Think, a model designed to think like a PhD scientist. And China has officially launched GLM5, the world's strongest open-source model built without using a single Nvidia GPU. Hey guys, Alfie here. Welcome to the AI Nexus. It feels like every major AI company got on a group chat and decided to drop their biggest updates on the exact same day. Bite Dance just released Cance 2.0. And to put it simply, this model might be too good. It's being called the first real Sora killer because it doesn't just generate a silent clip. It acts like a full-blown AI movie director. What makes Cance 2.0 a breakthrough is its quadal system. Most AI tools just take a text prompt and hope for the best. Seance allows you to feed it text, images, audio, and even reference video clips all at once. For example, [music] you can upload a photo of a character, a voice recording of them speaking, and a video of a specific dance move. The AI then synthesizes all of that into a 1080p cinematic clip where the character's lips perfectly match the audio and their movements follow realworld physics. It won the top spot on the seed video bench 2.0 for motion stability, meaning objects don't randomly morph or glitch like they used to. But this incredible power immediately triggered a massive legal war. Within 48 hours of launch, social media was flooded with pixelperfect videos of Spider-Man, Darth Vader, and Grou. On February 13th, [music] Disney and Paramount sent heavyduty cease and desist letters to Bite Dance. Disney's lawyers didn't hold back, calling the tool a virtual smash and grab of their intellectual property. They claim Bite Dance basically prepackaged the model with a pirated library of Hollywood characters, treating billion-dollar franchises like free clip art. It raises the ultimate question for the agentic era. If an AI can dream up your favorite superhero perfectly, who actually owns that dream? Let us know in the comments. The battle over creativity just exploded. But while Bite Dance is rewriting Hollywood, Open AAI is rewriting how software gets built. Open AAI has launched GPT 5.3 Codeex Spark. And it's designed to solve the single biggest problem in AI coding. If you've used AI to write code, you know the latency trap. You ask for a quick logic fix and then you're forced to sit there for 20 seconds watching the text slowly crawl across the screen. To fix this, Open AAI is splitting the world of AI work into two lanes. First, there is the long mission lane where agents plan and work on complex tasks for an hour. But the second lane is the tight realtime

seconds watching the text slowly crawl across the screen. To fix this, Open AAI is splitting the world of AI work into two lanes. First, there is the long mission lane where agents plan and work on complex tasks for an hour. But the second lane is the tight realtime who is actively typing and needs an assistant that moves as fast as their own thoughts. This is the Spark lane. To make Spark feel instant, OpenAI moved away from standard GPU clusters. Instead, they've partnered with Cerebras to run Spark on the wafer scale engine 3 W3. While most AI chips are the size of a postage stamp, the WSE3 is one massive continuous [music] piece of silicon packed with four trillion transistors. It's like a specialized drag car built for raw straight line speed. It's hitting over 1,000 tokens per second, meaning the code [music] doesn't just stream. It basically appears the moment you finish your request. But speed is only half of the strategy. Open AAI has officially acquired OpenClaw, the viral open-source framework that recently took over the developer [music] world. If you missed it, OpenClaw, formerly known as Clawedbot, is the engine behind Moltbook, that weird AI only social network where agents started forming their own cultures and even a religion. But beyond the memes, OpenClaw is a serious piece of tech. Unlike a standard chatbot that just talks, OpenClaw is a command-driven framework. It gives an AI hands on your local computer. It operates through a heartbeat system, waking itself up every few minutes to check your emails, monitor files, or run terminal commands without you ever touching a keyboard. You don't even need a browser. You just message it through WhatsApp or Telegram, and it executes [music] tasks directly on your machine. The future of this partnership is the digital employee, a system that doesn't just suggest how to book a flight or organize a database, but actually logs in and gets the job done autonomously. If you want deep, no hype breakdowns of the biggest AI [music] and humanoid robot breakthroughs every single week. This is the place. We don't just cover headlines, we break down what actually matters, subscribe to the AI Nexus and stay ahead of the curve. Google is moving to the other extreme with Gemini 3 deep think. If Spark is the fast talker, Deepthink is the slow researcher. This is Google's push into system 2 thinking. Instead of guessing the next word instantly, the model uses an internal thinking budget to iterate, verify, and prune its own logic before it speaks. This isn't a chatty update. It's a specialized mode for science, research, and engineering. Google is pitching this for problems that don't have a single right answer where data is messy and constraints are fuzzy. The benchmarks for deep think are frankly ridiculous. It hit an 84.6% 6% on ARC AGI2, which is widely considered the hardest test for true out-of-the-box

research, and engineering. Google is pitching this for problems that don't have a single right answer where data is messy and constraints are fuzzy. The benchmarks for deep think are frankly ridiculous. It hit an 84.6% 6% on ARC AGI2, which is widely considered the hardest test for true out-of-the-box achieved gold medal performance on the 2025 International Math Olympiad. But the most [music] realworld application is their sketch to 3D feature. You can draw a rough napkin [music] sketch of a part or a toy and Deepthink analyzes the geometry, builds the 3D model, and generates a print ready file. It's bridging the gap between [music] a fuzzy human idea and a physical artifact. It's now available for AI ultra subscribers positioned as a premium tool for scientists and engineers who care more about accuracy than a quick chat. Deepthink is about structured reasoning. But Anthropic is asking a different question. What if the model just remembers everything? While open AAI is racing for speed, Anthropic is going for massive context and reliability with Claude Opus 4.6. Anthropic released Claude Opus 4.6 and it's a direct shot at the intelligence crown. The big flex here is the 1 million token context window. You can now drop entire technical libraries or thousandpage legal documents into the prompt and it doesn't just read them, it understands them. The standout feature is adaptive thinking. Instead of you picking fast or smart mode, the model now dynamically decides how much brain power to use. If you ask a simple question, it snaps [music] back. If you ask it to find a memory leak in a massive code base, it pauses to think. And the results show it's currently the number one model on Terminal Bench 2.0 and leads the industry on humanity's last exam, a test designed to be so hard that humans [music] score 90% and most AIs fail. It also beats GPT 5.2 by 144 points on GDP vala, which measures high value knowledge work in law and finance. If you need a model that plans like an architect before it acts, Opus 4.6 is the new gold standard. So, the US labs are battling for intelligence and context. Meanwhile, China is fighting a completely different war, independence. Outside the US, the narrative is shifting toward what's being called sovereign AI. While the West focuses on proprietary models, China AI just dropped GLM5, a massive 744 billion parameter mixture of experts model. What makes this launch historic isn't just the sheer size. It is the hardware powering it. Jepoo trained this entire model on 100,000 Huawei Ascend 92010B chips without using a single Nvidia GPU. Since Jepu is on the US entity list, they've been cut off from high-end Western chips. So GLM5 is a major geopolitical statement. It proves that a Frontier class model can be built entirely on domestic Chinese hardware, breaking the reliance on American silicon. The architecture is built for what Japu calls agentic engineering. Out

US entity list, they've been cut off from high-end Western chips. So GLM5 is a major geopolitical statement. It proves that a Frontier class model can be built entirely on domestic Chinese hardware, breaking the reliance on American silicon. The architecture is built for what Japu calls agentic engineering. Out about 40 billion are active at any one time, allowing it to be fast and efficient despite its massive knowledge base. It currently sits as the strongest openweights model in the world, hitting a 77.8% on swbench verified, which puts it neck-and-neck with proprietary giants like Claude Opus. Released under a permissive MIT license, GLM5 is now the primary heavyweight for anyone building open-source agentic workflows that require high reliability without the high cost of American APIs. AI models are getting smarter by the hour. But while the software world levels up, the physical world is starting to move. Now, let's talk about humanoid robots. Now, let's talk about humanoid robots. Figure AI just dropped one of the most advanced robotic hands ever built, pushing closer to real human level dexterity. And at the same time, you can now actually buy an $8,000 robot that folds your laundry at home. Yeah, this is happening. Abot revealed the new Expedition A3 that moves like a trained fighter. While everything sounds exciting in robotics, Unitry G1's kung fu show hit an elderly man, raising serious questions. Hey guys, Alfie here. Welcome to AI Nexus. And this week in AI and robotics, breakthroughs are getting bigger, robots are getting faster, and the line between innovation and risk is getting thinner. Figure AAI has released its seventh generation robotic hand. CEO Brett Adcock shared that the team has been working on this design quietly for nearly 3 years. During that time, Figure has already built three different humanoid robot bodies, but reached its seventh hand version. That pace shows where the real difficulty lies. Building a robot that walks is difficult, but building a hand that works like a human hand is far more complex. The human hand remains one of the hardest challenges in robotics. A real hand has 27 degrees of freedom, meaning there are 27 independent ways the fingers and thumb can move. Most robots avoid that complexity and use simple grippers to pick up objects. Figure is taking a different path by focusing on true dexterity. The progress across earlier models shows how quickly the technology is improving. Figure01 started with basic gripping tools that could hold items but had limited movement. Figure 02 moved forward [music] with 16 degrees of freedom in each hand and the strength to lift about 55 pounds. Figure 03 introduced palm mounted cameras and highly sensitive touch sensors capable of detecting forces as light as 3 g, lighter than a small sugar packet. Now the seventh generation hand aims to move closer to human level performance. Early signs point to over 20° of freedom, smaller joint movements, stronger [music] motors, and more advanced touch

highly sensitive touch sensors capable of detecting forces as light as 3 g, lighter than a small sugar packet. Now the seventh generation hand aims to move closer to human level performance. Early signs point to over 20° of freedom, smaller joint movements, stronger [music] motors, and more advanced touch powerful. Pick up a fragile egg without cracking it. Fold clothing and then grip a tool for work tasks. Other companies are also racing toward this goal. Sanctuary AI already uses hands with 21 degrees of freedom. Figures AI system called Helix helps the robot learn tasks without needing new programming every time. Solving the hand problem could unlock real work in factories, kitchens, and homes. And while companies are racing to perfect robotic hands for complex work, another startup is taking a simpler approach, focusing on just one everyday task inside your home. Now you can finally buy a robot that folds your laundry. San Francisco based Weave Robotics has started selling its home laundry folding robot Isaac Zero. For now, orders are limited to Bay Area residents with deliveries beginning this month. The robot costs $8,000 if bought outright, or customers can choose a $450 monthly subscription instead. Isaac Zero is designed to handle one simple task at home, folding clean laundry. The robot is stationary and plugs into a standard wall outlet. After washing and drying clothes, the user places a pile of laundry in front of the machine, and walks away. Over the next 30 to 90 minutes, Isaac Zero sorts through the clothes, folds each item, and leaves everything stacked neatly. The process is simple and hands-free, but it takes time and is not instant. There are also clear limitations. Isaac Zero cannot fold large blankets or bed sheets, and it often struggles with clothes that are turned inside out. Even though it is presented as an advanced home robot, Isaac Zero is not fully independent. When the system gets confused or makes a mistake, remote operators at Weave Robotics can briefly step in using the robot's built-in cameras. These operators help for about 5 to 10 seconds before returning control to the machine, which means a real person may momentarily see into the room while assisting the task. Weave Robotics describes Isaac Zero as a learning system that improves over time. As Isaac Zero folds more laundry, its software [music] is designed to become more accurate and reliable. Still, for $8,000, the robot is focused on just one basic job supported by human backup and can take up to 90 minutes per load. It shows how home robots are starting to enter everyday life, even if the technology is still in its early stages. So, from folding clothes quietly in your living room, let's jump to something far more explosive. Humanoid robots now performing full martial arts routines on stage. Agibbot revealed a preview of the Expedition A3 humanoid robot through a kung fu demo that spread rapidly across

still in its early stages. So, from folding clothes quietly in your living room, let's jump to something far more explosive. Humanoid robots now performing full martial arts routines on stage. Agibbot revealed a preview of the Expedition A3 humanoid robot through a kung fu demo that spread rapidly across Agibbot A3 moves with the speed and rhythm of a trained fighter instead of the stiff motion people expect from a machine. Agibot A3 stands about 5'9 in tall, which is close to human height. So the movement feels natural and easy to connect with right away. In the video, Agibbot A3 performs fast midair sidekicks, then links them into smooth side flips with light touches on the floor before ending with clean back flips. A person nearby steps back like in a dodge drill, which makes the action feel more real and sharp. The flips happen in about half a second, and there are no wires or support frames holding Agbot A3. Balance control is doing the hard work, keeping the robot stable while spinning, landing, and changing direction. Agabot A3 has more than 40 moving joints across the body, and the leg motors can produce up to about 300 Newton meters of torquy. That is strong force for fast and controlled kicks. To stay steady, Agabot A3 uses cameras, lidar, and balance sensors to track motion and keep footing. The software studies large training data to predict safe landing points and quickly corrects movement if a wobble begins. Ajabot, also known as Juwan Robotics, is based in Shanghai and started in 2023 by former Huawei engineers. Ajibot has even trained movement patterns with Shaolin Temple monks, blending traditional martial arts stances with modern control. Expedition A3 is still marked as an upcoming model. So, this demo is only a preview, not a product on sale yet. But as robots become faster and more athletic, one big question follows. What happens when something goes wrong in a live crowd? While there's been a lot of exciting news about robot performances and new capabilities, safety concerns are still growing. Unit's G1, known for its agility and kung fu style athletic [music] movements, was recently involved in an incident that raised serious questions about public safety during live demos. G1 was performing a fast-paced kung fu routine in front of a public crowd designed to show balance, agility, and explosive kicking power. During the performance, a nearby child stepped close to the edge of the demo space just as the robot launched a high front kick. The G1's foot passed only about 8 to 12 in from the child's head. There was no contact, but the distance was extremely close. The child ducked instinctively while the robot continued the routine without slowing down or adjusting mid-motion. Shortly after, an elderly man unexpectedly stepped into the demonstration area. At that exact moment, the robot extended a fast sidekick with its leg moving at roughly 4.5 mph. The impact struck the man

The child ducked instinctively while the robot continued the routine without slowing down or adjusting mid-motion. Shortly after, an elderly man unexpectedly stepped into the demonstration area. At that exact moment, the robot extended a fast sidekick with its leg moving at roughly 4.5 mph. The impact struck the man him to fall immediately. Reports later confirmed he was hospitalized overnight with rib bruising and shock, though no fractures were found. What unsettled many observers was the robot's response. Unitry G1 did not pause, react, or trigger an emergency stop in either situation. The system continued executing its programmed routine exactly as designed, focused on completing the sequence smoothly and accurately. Unitry G1 is built for athletic movement using high torque joints, reinforcement learning, and precise force control across more than 20° of freedom. In performance mode, the robot prioritizes stability and completing the planned motion. Its cameras and LAR are better at detecting larger fixed obstacles, which can make [music] quick human movement near the robot harder to respond to in real time. This incident highlights a growing concern as humanoid robots become faster and more powerful. In crowded public settings without strict barriers, even a small gap in awareness can create real risk, especially when high-speed robotic motion is happening just inches away from spectators. Incidents like this highlight a deeper issue. Not just how fast robots move, but how precisely they sense, react, and control force. And that brings us back to robotic hands. Budapest [music] based startup Alonic is building a robotic hand designed to work more like a real human [music] hand. The company calls this method 3D tissue braiding, allowing the hand to move more naturally. The startup recently raised $7.2 million in a funding round led by Visionaries Club. With support from several other investors, Alonic is tackling one of the biggest challenges in robotics. Building a highly dextrous robotic hand. Today, creating a hand with precise movement is slow, complex, and expensive. Engineers often have to assemble hundreds of tiny screws, bearings, and rigid joints by hand, and the process can take weeks to complete. Even after all that time and effort, some robotic hands can cost over $25,000 and still remain fragile with a higher risk of wearing out or breaking under repeated stress. Alonic replaces this complex assembly process with a single automated weaving system. The company's machine wraps high strength fibers around a lightweight internal frame, similar to how human tendons wrap around bones. During the process, elastic materials and sensors are braided together while artificial tendons are threaded through the structure. This design spreads force evenly across the surface of the hand. As a result, the robotic hand can grip objects with different shapes and stiffness levels more naturally. This approach saves a huge amount of time. A structure that once required weeks to assemble, can now be produced in minutes. The hand's main structure, excluding motors, could cost as little as $50. Alonic is already

robotic hand can grip objects with different shapes and stiffness levels more naturally. This approach saves a huge amount of time. A structure that once required weeks to assemble, can now be produced in minutes. The hand's main structure, excluding motors, could cost as little as $50. Alonic is already robotics companies and has completed a pilot project in electronics manufacturing. With the new funding, Alonic plans to grow beyond its current 15 person team and develop second generation machines that are five times faster, twice as compact, and offer three times higher resolution than the first version. While some companies are redesigning the mechanics of dexterity from the inside out, others are proving what full body coordination already looks like on stage. AGIBOT's robot gala already set the tone this week with a major robotic performance, but another Chinese company also created a standout moment. Rawa marked the 2026 Lunar New Year with a live stage show featuring its L7 humanoid robot. Performing a full traditional Chinese sword dance, executing high-speed martial arts sequences while holding a real sword, the L7 delivered sharp strikes, deep stances, and fast directional changes that demanded full body coordination, balance, and precise motion control. The performance was more than cultural entertainment. It worked as a demanding stress test for the robot's balance system. Every sword swing required steady foot placement. Every spin and rapid transition pushed the robot's center of gravity to its limit. Maintaining stability during explosive movements shows how advanced humanoid control systems have become. Robotera designed the L7 to be one of the fastest humanoid robots in its class. The robot can sprint at nearly 9 mph on two legs. The L7 stands about 5' 7 in tall and weighs around 143 lb. It features 55 degrees of freedom, allowing smooth and flexible movement across the entire body. By mixing traditional sword moves with advanced robotics, Robotera showed that fast humanoid movement is becoming more stable and reliable. From high-speed sword dances to realworld animal handling, humanoid robots are now stepping out of control demos and into unpredictable environments. Xping recently released a Lunar New Year video that quickly spread across the internet, and the reason is clear. In the video, Xping's Iron Humanoid robot calmly walks up to a real living horse and handles it with the confidence of an experienced rancher. This is not computer graphics or a stage delusion. Iron holds the [music] lead rope, walks beside the horse at a steady pace, and gently touches the horse's muzzle without [music] causing fear or panic. For that moment to work, many complex systems have to operate perfectly together. Iron must measure exactly how much tension to apply to the rope so it does not pull too hard or too lightly. The robot has to stay balanced on uneven ground while the horse shifts weight and changes direction. At the same time, the AI system must read the horse's body language in real time. If the horse

apply to the rope so it does not pull too hard or too lightly. The robot has to stay balanced on uneven ground while the horse shifts weight and changes direction. At the same time, the AI system must read the horse's body language in real time. If the horse respond instantly. One sudden mistake could cause the horse, which can weigh over 1,000 lbs, to bolt or kick. Xpung designed Iron with 3,000 tops of computing power, giving the robot the ability to process huge amounts of data every second. The hands include force feedback sensors, allowing Iron to feel how firmly it is gripping. The artificial joints and spine help the robot move smoothly and adjust posture as the horse moves. In another public demo, Xpong even showed Iron falling, proving the machine is real and still learning, not a person [music] in a costume. Xpong plans to deploy iron in factories, retail stores, and eventually homes by late 2026. If iron can safely guide a,000lb animal today, it signals that humanoid robots are beginning to operate in the unpredictable environments of the real
